title: Greetings and Farewells
description: Basic greetings and farewells for daily interactions
theory:
👋 Greetings & Farewells Masterclass

Greetings are more than just words; they set the tone for every social and professional interaction. Understanding the nuances of each phrase is vital.

1. The Formal Spectrum

Use these in business, interviews, or when meeting someone for the first time.

Greeting IPA Usage Context
Good morning /ɡʊd ˈmɔːrnɪŋ/ From sunrise until 12:00 PM.
Good afternoon /ɡʊd ˌæftərˈnuːn/ From 12:00 PM until 6:00 PM.
How do you do? /haʊ duː juː duː/ Very formal. Only used the first time you meet someone.
2. Casual & Slang Greetings

Perfect for friends, colleagues you know well, and relaxed environments.

  • "What's up?" → Very casual. Often answered with "Not much."
  • "How's it going?" → A friendly way to ask about someone's life.
  • "Hey there!" → Warm and welcoming.
🚀 Advanced Rule: The 'Goodnight' Trap

Never use "Goodnight" as a greeting. It is strictly a departure phrase.

  • ✅ Arriving at 8 PM: "Good evening!"
  • ✅ Leaving at 8 PM: "Goodnight!"

title: Personal Introduction
description: How to introduce yourself and ask about others
theory:
🆔 Personal Information

Introducing yourself is more than just saying your name. It forms the foundation for networking and connecting with others in a new environment.

📝 Key Questions & Answers
Question Meaning Typical Answer
What's your name? ¿Cómo te llamas? My name is... / I'm...
Where are you from? ¿De dónde eres? I'm from Colombia.
How old are you? ¿Cuántos años tienes? I am 25 years old.
🚀 Professional Tip: "I have" vs "I am"

In Spanish, we say "Tengo 25 años" (I have). In English, age is a state of being, not a possession. Always use the verb To Be.

  • ✅ I am 25 years old.
  • ❌ I have 25 years.

title: The Alphabet
theory:
Mastering the English Alphabet

The English alphabet is the foundation of literacy. Beyond simple letters, mastering the pronunciation and phonetic spelling is crucial for professional communication.

Letter IPA Letter IPA
A /eɪ/ B /biː/
C /siː/ D /diː/
E /iː/ F /ɛf/
G /dʒiː/ H /eɪtʃ/
I /aɪ/ J /dʒeɪ/
K /keɪ/ L /ɛl/
M /ɛm/ N /ɛn/
O /oʊ/ P /piː/
Q /kjuː/ R /ɑːr/
S /ɛs/ T /tiː/
U /juː/ V /viː/
W /ˈdʌbəl.juː/ X /ɛks/
Y /waɪ/ Z /zɛd/ (UK) /ziː/ (US)
🚀 Professional Spelling (Symbols)

In business English, you must know how to spell symbols for email addresses:

  • @ (At)
  • . (Dot)
  • - (Hyphen)
  • _ (Underscore)

title: Numbers
description: Cardinal and ordinal numbers up to 10,000
theory:
🔢 Numbers 1-100

Numbers are essential for prices, ages, dates, and phone numbers. Mastering their pronunciation prevents costly miscommunications.

🚀 The Suffix Secret: -TEEN vs -TY

Many students confuse 13 with 30, or 14 with 40. Here is the key:

Suffix Rule Example
-TEEN (13-19) Long and stressed at the end. Thir-TEEN, Four-TEEN
-TY (20-90) Short, sharp, and unstressed. THIR-ty, FOR-ty
📝 Building Complex Numbers

For numbers greater than 20, simply join the tens and units with a hyphen:

  • 25 → Twenty-five
  • 42 → Forty-two
  • 88 → Eighty-eight

title: Subject Pronouns
description: I, you, he, she, it, we, they
theory:
👤 Subject Pronouns Masterclass

Subject pronouns replace nouns to avoid repetition. They are the "actors" of your sentences.

📌 The Pronoun Grid
Pronoun Meaning IPA
I Yo (Always capitalized) /aɪ/
You Tú / Usted / Ustedes (Singular & Plural) /juː/
He Él (Male persons) /hiː/
She Ella (Female persons) /ʃiː/
It Ello (Things, animals, concepts, weather) /ɪt/
We Nosotros /wiː/
They Ellos / Ellas (Plural for people AND things) /ðeɪ/
⚠️ The "IT" Mandatory Rule

In Spanish, we can drop the subject (e.g., "Llueve"). In English, a sentence MUST have a subject. We use "It" as a dummy subject.

  • ✅ It is raining.
  • ❌ Is raining.

title: Verb "To Be"
description: Am, is, are — usage and forms
theory:
✨ The Verb "To Be"

The verb "To Be" is the backbone of the English language. It expresses existence (who you are) and state (how or where you are). Mastering its pronunciation is the first step to fluency.

📊 Conjugation & Phonetics

Native speakers rarely use the full forms in conversation. Focus on the contractions.

Pronoun Full Form Contraction Negative
I am I'm I'm not
You are You're You aren't
He/She/It is He's / She's / It's isn't
We/They are We're / They're aren't
🚀 Professional Tip: The "It's" Confusion

Do not confuse It's (It is) with Its (Possessive).

  • ✅ It's a beautiful day. (It is)
  • ✅ The company doubled its profits. (Possessive)

title: Wh- Questions
description: Who, what, where, when, why, how
theory:
❓ Wh- Questions

These are question words used to ask for specific information, not just a "Yes" or "No" answer.

🔍 The Wh- Words
Wh- Word Usage Example
Who For people Who is that man?
What For things/actions What is your name?
Where For places Where are you from?
When For time When is the meeting?
Why For reasons Why are you late?
How For manner/condition How are you?
🚀 How + Adjective

"How" can combine with adjectives for specific questions:

  • How old: For age (How old are you?)
  • How much: For price/quantity (How much is it?)

title: Demonstratives
theory:
👉 Demonstratives (This, That, These, Those)

Demonstratives show where an object, event, or person is in relation to the speaker. They depend on two things: Distance and Quantity.

📌 The Demonstrative Grid
Distance Singular (1) Plural (2+)
Close (Aquí) This (Este/Esta) These (Estos/Estas)
Far (Allá) That (Ese/Aquel) Those (Esos/Aquellos)
🚀 Pronunciation Trick

It's crucial to pronounce "This" and "These" differently to avoid confusion:

  • This: Short sound, like a quick 'i'. /ðɪs/
  • These: Long sound, like a smiling 'ii'. /ðiːz/

title: Possessives
description: My, your, his, her, its, our, their
theory:
🏠 Possessives

Possessive adjectives show ownership. They always sit directly in front of the noun they describe.

🔗 Subject to Possessive Mapping
Subject Possessive Example
IMyMy laptop
YouYourYour office
HeHisHis project
SheHerHer email
ItItsIts battery
WeOurOur team
TheyTheirTheir budget
🚀 Professional Nuance: Their vs There vs They're

These three words sound exactly the same (/ðeər/) but have entirely different meanings:

  • Their: Possessive (Their house).
  • There: Location (Over there).
  • They're: Contraction of "They are" (They're coming).

title: What Time Is It?
description: Telling and asking for the time
theory:
⏱️ What Time Is It?

There are two primary ways to tell the time in English: the "Digital" way and the "Traditional" way.

1. Digital vs Traditional
Time Digital (Easy) Traditional (Native)
10:00 Ten o'clock Ten o'clock
10:15 Ten fifteen A quarter past ten
10:30 Ten thirty Half past ten
10:45 Ten forty-five A quarter to eleven
🚀 Advanced Rule: AM vs PM

English speakers rarely use the 24-hour clock in conversation. We use AM (morning) and PM (afternoon/night).

  • 14:00 → 2:00 PM
  • 20:00 → 8:00 PM

title: Prepositions of Time
description: In, on, at — using prepositions with time
theory:
Theory: Jobs & Professions

Describing career paths and employment roles is fundamental for professional networking.

1. Professional Roles
  • Healthcare: Doctor, Nurse, Surgeon.
  • Education: Teacher, Professor, Tutor.
  • Office: Manager, Secretary, Accountant.
  • Manual: Plumber, Electrician, Carpenter.
🚀 Advanced Rule: A/An with Jobs

In English, you must always use an indefinite article before a job title in the singular.

  • ✅ I am a lawyer.
  • ❌ I am lawyer.

title: Simple Present
theory:
Theory: Workplace Vocabulary

Key terminology for office environments, equipment, and daily operations.

1. Office Equipment
  • Hardware: Desktop, Laptop, Keyboard, Monitor.
  • Furniture: Desk, Ergonomic chair, Filing cabinet.
  • Supplies: Stapler, Paper clips, Highlighter.
🚀 Advanced Rule: Colleagues vs Coworkers

Both terms are common. "Colleague" is slightly more formal and often used in professional environments.

title: Adverbs of Frequency
theory:
Theory: Singular & Plural Nouns

Pluralization follows specific phonetic and spelling rules based on the ending of the noun.

1. Standard Spelling Rules
Ending Rule Example
General Add -s Book → Books
-s, -sh, -ch, -x, -z Add -es Bus → Buses
Consonant + y Change y to -ies City → Cities
🚀 Advanced Rule: Irregular Plurals

Some nouns change completely or not at all:

  • Person → People
  • Man → Men
  • Tooth → Teeth
  • Sheep → Sheep (No change)

title: Comparatives
theory:
Theory: Articles (A / An)

Indefinite articles are used with singular, countable nouns when they are non-specific.

1. Sound vs Spelling

The choice between A and An depends on the sound of the next word, not the letter.

  • A: Before a consonant sound. (A car, A pen).
  • An: Before a vowel sound. (An egg, An apple).
🚀 Advanced Rule: Tricky H and U
  • An hour: The 'H' is silent, so it starts with a vowel sound.
  • A university: The 'U' sounds like 'You', which is a consonant sound.

title: Food
theory:
Theory: Demonstratives (This, That, These, Those)

Demonstratives indicate the physical or temporal distance of a noun from the speaker.

1. Distance Matrix
Distance Singular Plural
Near (Here) This These
Far (There) That Those
🚀 Advanced Rule: Pronunciation

Distinguish between the short sound in This [ðɪs] and the long sound in These [ðiːz].

title: Likes and Dislikes
theory:
Theory: Classroom Vocabulary

Essential terminology for academic environments, including physical objects and common actions.

1. Objects and Furniture
  • Writing: Pen, Pencil, Chalk, Marker.
  • Surface: Desk, Whiteboard, Blackboard.
  • Storage: Backpack, Pencil case, Locker.
🚀 Advanced Rule: Verbs for Class
  • Take a test: To complete an exam.
  • Fail: Not achieving the required score.
  • Pass: Achieving the required score.

title: Present Continuous
theory:
Theory: Basic Adjectives

Adjectives provide descriptions. In English, they follow specific placement and form rules.

1. Position in Sentence

Unlike some other languages, adjectives in English usually come before the noun.

  • ✅ The blue house.
  • ❌ The house blue.
🚀 Advanced Rule: No Plurals

Adjectives in English are never pluralized, even if the noun is plural.

  • ✅ Green apples.
  • ❌ Greens apples.

title: Simple vs. Continuous
description: When to use simple vs. continuous tenses
theory:
Theory: Colors

Colors are fundamental descriptors. Beyond basic shades, English uses compounds for precision.

1. Compound Colors

Use "Light" or "Dark" before a color to indicate intensity:

  • Light blue / Dark blue.
  • Pale green / Deep red.
🚀 Advanced Rule: Spelling Variation

There is a regional difference in spelling:

  • Color / Gray: American English (US).
  • Colour / Grey: British English (UK).

title: Simple Past
theory:
Theory: Telling the Time

There are two primary ways to tell the time in English: the "Digital" way and the "Traditional" way.

1. Digital vs Traditional
Time Digital Traditional
10:15 Ten fifteen A quarter past ten
10:30 Ten thirty Half past ten
10:45 Ten forty-five A quarter to eleven
🚀 Advanced Rule: AM vs PM

English speakers rarely use the 24-hour clock in conversation. We use AM (morning) and PM (afternoon/night).

title: Describing Places
description: Vocabulary and phrases for describing locations
theory:
Theory: Days & Months

Formatting dates and discussing schedules requires correct capitalization and preposition usage.

1. Prepositions for Time
  • ON: Used for specific days. (On Monday, On June 5th).
  • IN: Used for months or years. (In July, In 2024).
🚀 Advanced Rule: Capitalization

Days and Months are Proper Nouns in English and must always be capitalized.

  • ✅ Monday / ❌ monday

title: Giving Directions
description: Finding your way and helping others
theory:
Theory: Present Simple (Positive)

The Present Simple describes habits, routines, and permanent states.

1. The 3rd Person Singular Rule

For He, She, It, we must add an -S to the verb.

  • I / You / We / They → work.
  • He / She / It → works.
🚀 Advanced Rule: Spelling Variants
  • Verbs ending in -ch, -sh, -s, -x → Add -es (Watch → Watches).
  • Verbs ending in -y (after consonant) → -ies (Study → Studies).

title: Superlatives
theory:
Theory: Present Simple (Negative & Questions)

Unlike the verb "To Be," action verbs require the auxiliary DO / DOES for negatives and questions.

1. Using the Auxiliary
  • I do not (don't) work.
  • He does not (doesn't) work.
🚀 Advanced Rule: The "Ghost" S

When you use DOES or DOESN'T, the main verb loses its -S.

  • Affirmative: He works.
  • Question: Does he work? (Not: Does he works?)

title: Present Perfect
description: Life experiences with have/has + past participle
theory:
Theory: Daily Routine

Combining verbs and time expressions to describe a typical day.

1. Phrasal Verbs in Routine
  • Wake up: To stop sleeping.
  • Get up: To leave the bed.
  • Go to bed: To start sleeping.
🚀 Advanced Rule: Sequence Markers

Use these words to link your routine:

First, Then, After that, Finally.

title: Present Perfect vs. Past
description: When to use present perfect vs simple past
theory:
Theory: Adverbs of Frequency

These adverbs indicate how often an action occurs.

1. The Frequency Scale
  • Always (100%)
  • Usually (80%)
  • Often (60%)
  • Sometimes (40%)
  • Rarely / Seldom (10%)
  • Never (0%)
🚀 Advanced Rule: Position

Frequency adverbs go before main verbs, but after the verb "To Be."

  • I always eat breakfast.
  • I am always on time.

title: Already, Yet, Since & For
description: Time adverbs used with present perfect
theory:
Theory: Can / Can't (Ability)

Modal verbs like "Can" express ability or possibility and do not change form.

1. Universal Conjugation

Unlike other verbs, "Can" is the same for all subjects:

  • I can swim.
  • He can swim. (No -S!)
🚀 Advanced Rule: Pronunciation

Focus on the vowel sound. "Can" [kæn] is short and weak. "Can't" [kɑːnt] is long and stressed.

title: Describing People
theory:
Theory: Like, Love, Hate (+ -ing)

Verbs of preference are usually followed by another verb in the Gerund (-ing) form.

1. Preferences
  • I love reading.
  • She likes dancing.
  • They hate waiting.
🚀 Advanced Rule: Spelling the -ing

If a verb ends in 'e', remove it before adding -ing:

Dance → Dancing.

title: Modal Verbs
description: Can, could, should, must and their uses
theory:
Theory: Object Pronouns

Object pronouns receive the action of the verb and follow the verb or a preposition.

1. Mapping Subjects to Objects
  • I → Me
  • You → You
  • He → Him
  • She → Her
  • It → It
  • We → Us
  • They → Them
🚀 Advanced Rule: Placement

Always place them after the verb: "I love it." (Not: I it love).

title: Future
description: Will and Be going to for future plans
theory:
Theory: Food & Drink

Categorizing food into Countable and Uncountable nouns is key for correct grammar.

1. Categories
  • Fruit/Veg: Apple, Banana, Carrot.
  • Dairy: Milk, Cheese, Yogurt.
  • Meat: Chicken, Beef, Pork.
🚀 Advanced Rule: Meal Times

In English, we typically have Breakfast, Lunch, and Dinner. We do not use "the" before meals (e.g., "I have breakfast").

title: Giving Messages
theory:
Theory: Some & Any

These quantifiers are used to discuss indefinite quantities of plural or uncountable nouns.

1. Usage Patterns
Sentence Type Quantifier
Affirmative (+) Some
Negative (-) Any
Question (?) Any
🚀 Advanced Rule: Exceptions

We use SOME in questions when offering or requesting something.

  • "Would you like some coffee?" (Offer).

title: Talking About Changes
description: How things have changed over time
theory:
Theory: Countable vs Uncountable

Understanding the nature of nouns determines whether we can use numbers or plural forms.

1. Key Differences
  • Countable: Can be counted (1 apple, 2 apples). Can be plural.
  • Uncountable: Cannot be counted (Water, Rice). No plural form.
🚀 Advanced Rule: Units of Measurement

To count uncountable things, we use "containers" or units:

  • A glass of water.
  • A kilogram of rice.

title: There was / There were
description: Past existence of things and people
theory:
🔙 Pasado Simple (Verbos Regulares)

El Pasado Simple se usa para acciones que empezaron y terminaron en el pasado. La mayoría de los verbos en inglés son "regulares".

📏 La Regla del -ED

Para formar el pasado de un verbo regular, simplemente añadimos -ed al final.

  • Walk → Walked
  • Play → Played
✍️ Reglas de Ortografía
  • Terminados en E: Solo añade -d. Love → Loved.
  • Consonante + Y: Cambia la Y por -ied. Study → Studied.
  • CVC (Consonante-Vocal-Consonante): Duplica la última letra. Stop → Stopped.

title: Used to + infinitive
description: Past habits that are no longer true
theory:
📋 Pasado Simple (Verbos Irregulares)

Los verbos irregulares no siguen la regla del "-ed". Tienen su propia forma única que debemos memorizar.

💡 Verbos más comunes
Infinitivo Pasado Significado
Go Went Ir / Fue
Eat Ate Comer / Comió
See Saw Ver / Vio
Buy Bought Comprar / Compró
📝 Ejemplos

"I went to the park yesterday." (Fui al parque ayer)

"She ate a delicious pizza." (Ella comió una pizza deliciosa)

title: Past Simple vs. Past Continuous
theory:
🚫 Pasado Simple (Negativo y Preguntas)

Al igual que en el presente usábamos "do/does", en el pasado usamos el auxiliar **DID**.

🛠️ El Auxiliar: DID

Cuando aparece DID o DIDN'T, el verbo vuelve a su forma básica (¡pierde el pasado!).

  • Afirmativo: I went.
  • Negativo: I didn't go. (No se dice "didn't went")
❓ Preguntas

Estructura: DID + Sujeto + Verbo (base) + ?

"Did you see the movie?"

"Did she buy the bread?"

title: Comparisons and Evaluations
theory:
🔄 Hábito en el Pasado: Used To

Se usa para hablar de cosas que hacías antes con frecuencia, pero que ya no haces.

🏗️ Estructura

Sujeto + used to + verbo básico

"I used to smoke, but I quit." (Yo solía fumar, pero lo dejé)

"She used to live in Paris." (Ella solía vivir en París)

⚠️ Negación y Pregunta

Usamos el auxiliar DID, por lo que el "used" pierde la 'd':

"I didn't use to exercise." (No solía hacer ejercicio)

"Did you use to have long hair?"

title: Wish + Past Tense
theory:
⚖️ Adjetivos Comparativos

Sirven para comparar dos cosas o personas. Dependiendo del largo del adjetivo, la regla cambia.

📏 Reglas de Comparación
  • Cortos (1 sílaba): Añade -er. Fast → Faster.
  • Largos (2+ sílabas): Añade MORE antes. Beautiful → More beautiful.
  • Terminados en Y: Cambia Y por -ier. Happy → Happier.
📝 La palabra mágica: THAN

Siempre usamos THAN (que) después del comparativo.

"The car is faster than the bike."

"Gold is more expensive than silver."

title: Indirect Questions
description: Polite ways to ask for information
theory:
🏆 Adjetivos Superlativos

Se usan para decir que algo es "el más..." de un grupo.

📏 Reglas Superlativas
  • Cortos: Añade THE + verbo + -est. The tallest.
  • Largos: Añade THE MOST antes. The most intelligent.
🌟 Irregulares Importantes
  • Good → The Best (El mejor).
  • Bad → The Worst (El peor).
  • Far → The Farthest (El más lejano).

(I wonder...)", "a": "I wonder if the bank is open"}, {"q": "What time is it? (Can you tell me...)", "a": "Can you tell me what time it is?"}, {"q": "Why did they leave? (Do you have any idea...)", "a": "Do you have any idea why they left?"}]}]}

title: Adverbs of Time
theory:
📊 Adverbios de Frecuencia

Indican qué tan seguido hacemos algo.

📉 Escala de Frecuencia
  • Always (100%), Usually (90%), Often (70%).
  • Sometimes (50%), Rarely (10%), Never (0%).
⚠️ Posición en la frase

Van **antes** del verbo principal, pero **después** del verbo To Be.

Correcto: "I always drink coffee."

Correcto: "I am always happy."

title: Future: be going to vs. will
theory:
🕒 Expresiones de Tiempo en Pasado

Palabras clave que nos ayudan a ubicar la acción en el tiempo pasado.

📅 Palabras Clave
  • Yesterday: Ayer.
  • Last (week/month/year): La semana/mes/año pasado.
  • Ago: Hace (se pone al final). Two days ago.
📝 Ejemplos

"I saw him two hours ago." (Lo vi hace dos horas)

"We traveled last summer." (Viajamos el verano pasado)

It ______ rain.", "options": ["is going to", "will"], "a": "is going to", "hint": "There is evidence (clouds)."}, {"q": "I'm hungry. title: Modal Verbs for Necessity
description: Must, have to, and their negatives
theory:
⚡ Presente Continuo

Se usa para acciones que están ocurriendo **ahora mismo** o en este periodo de tiempo.

🏗️ Estructura (Sujeto + To Be + -ing)

¡No olvides el verbo To Be!

  • I am studying.
  • You are working.
  • She is sleeping.
📝 Ejemplo

"I am watching TV right now." (Estoy viendo TV ahora mismo)

title: Modal Verbs for Requests
theory:
⚖️ Presente Continuo vs Simple

Saber cuándo usar uno u otro es la clave para hablar como un nativo.

🔍 La Diferencia Principal
  • Presente Simple: Rutinas y hechos permanentes. "I live in London."
  • Presente Continuo: Acciones temporales ocurriendo ahora. "I am sitting in a cafe."
💡 Palabras que ayudan

Simple: Usually, Always, Every day.

Continuo: Now, At the moment, Today.

title: Phrasal Verbs - Separable and Non-Separable
theory:
🚀 Futuro con "Going To"

Se usa para hablar de **planes e intenciones** que ya hemos decidido, o para **predicciones** basadas en evidencia física.

🏗️ Estructura (To Be + Going to + Verbo)
  • I am going to travel.
  • He is going to cook.
  • They are going to study.
📝 Ejemplos

"I am going to visit my parents next week." (Plan decidido)

"Look at the clouds! It is going to rain." (Predicción con evidencia)

Please ______ the lights.", "a": "turn on"}, {"q": "It's cold. ______ your coat.", "a": "Put on"}, {"q": "The plane is ready to ______.", "a": "take off"}, {"q": "Can you ______ my cat while I'm away?", "a": "look after"}]}]}

title: Quantifiers
description: Some, any, much, many, a lot of
theory:
✨ Futuro con "Will"

Se usa para decisiones tomadas en el momento, promesas, o predicciones basadas en opinión.

🏗️ Estructura

Sujeto + Will + Verbo (base)

  • Positive: I will (I'll) help you.
  • Negative: I will not (Won't) go.
  • Question: Will you marry me?
💡 Usos Clave

Decisión instantánea: "The phone is ringing. I'll answer it!"

Promesa: "I will always love you."

title: Relative Clauses (who, which, that)
description: Connecting and describing with relative pronouns
theory:
🤔 Sugerencias con "Shall"

Se usa principalmente en preguntas con **I** o **We** para ofrecer ayuda o hacer sugerencias.

🏗️ Estructura de Sugerencia

"Shall we go to the cinema?" (¿Y si vamos al cine?)

"Shall I open the window?" (¿Quieres que abra la ventana?)

⚠️ Nota cultural

"Shall" suena un poco más formal y educado que "Should". Es muy común en el inglés británico.

She lives in New York. (I have a friend...)", "a": "I have a friend who lives in New York"}, {"q": "He lost the watch. I gave it to him. (He lost the watch...)", "a": "He lost the watch that I gave to him"}, {"q": "The girl is my sister. She is wearing the hat. (The girl...)", "a": "The girl who is wearing the hat is my sister"}, {"q": "This is the book. It won the prize. (This is the book...)", "a": "This is the book which won the prize"}, {"q": "I know a place. We can eat there. (I know a place...)", "a": "I know a place where we can eat"}]}]}

title: Gerunds vs. Infinitives
description: When to use -ing vs to + verb
theory:
🚫 Obligación y Prohibición: Must / Mustn't

Se usa para obligaciones fuertes (a menudo internas) y reglas estrictas.

🏗️ Usos Principales
  • Must: Obligación fuerte. "I must study harder."
  • Mustn't: Prohibición. "You mustn't smoke here."
⚠️ ¡Cuidado!

Mustn't NO significa "no tienes que", significa **"está prohibido"**. Si algo es opcional, usamos "don't have to".

title: Zero Conditional
theory:
👔 Necesidad: Have to / Don't have to

Se usa para obligaciones externas (leyes, reglas del trabajo, deberes escolares).

🏗️ Estructura
  • Have to: Obligación. "I have to wear a uniform."
  • Don't have to: No es necesario (opcional). "You don't have to come if you're busy."
📝 Ejemplo

"She has to finish the report by tomorrow." (Obligación externa)
practice: {"games": [{"title": "Practice 1: Causes and Effects", "instruction": "Match the cause to its logical effect (Zero Conditional).", "type": "multiple_choice", "questions": [{"q": "If you heat ice, it ______.", "options": ["melts", "melted"], "a": "melts", "hint": "Zero conditional is used for universal truths (Present Simple)."}, {"q": "If it rains, the grass ______ wet.", "options": ["gets", "is getting"], "a": "gets", "hint": "Result in simple present."}, {"q": "If you don't eat, you ______ hungry.", "options": ["get", "got"], "a": "get", "hint": "A general fact."}, {"q": "If you touch a fire, you ______ burned.", "options": ["get", "gets"], "a": "get", "hint": "Result of a general action."}, {"q": "If babies are hungry, they ______.", "options": ["cry", "cried"], "a": "cry", "hint": "A natural reaction."}]}, {"title": "Practice 2: Correcting the Facts", "instruction": "Complete the Zero Conditional sentences.", "type": "fill_in", "questions": [{"q": "If you mix red and white, you ______ pink.", "a": "get"}, {"q": "Water boils if it ______ 100 degrees Celsius.", "a": "reaches"}, {"q": "If you ______ (not/water) plants, they die.", "a": "don't water"}, {"q": "Wood floats if you ______ it in water.", "a": "put"}, {"q": "If I ______ (stay) up late, I feel tired the next day.", "a": "stay"}]}]}

title: First Conditional
theory:
💡 Consejos: Should / Shouldn't

Se usa para dar o pedir consejos y opiniones. No es una obligación, es una recomendación.

🏗️ Estructura

Sujeto + Should + Verbo (base)

  • Consejo: "You should drink more water."
  • Pregunta: "What should I do?"
📝 Ejemplo

"You shouldn't stay up so late." (No deberías quedarte despierto hasta tan tarde)

title: Dates (Fechas)
theory:
📢 Imperativos (Órdenes e Instrucciones)

Se usan para dar órdenes directas, instrucciones, advertencias o invitaciones.

🏗️ Estructura (Sin Sujeto)

El imperativo es simplemente el verbo solo al principio de la frase.

  • Afirmativo: "Open the door!"
  • Negativo: "Don't touch that!"
💡 Modales de cortesía

Para que no suene grosero, solemos añadir "Please".

"Sit down, please."

title: Tag Questions (Preguntas Coletilla)
description: Confirmation questions at the end of statements
theory:
🎯 Infinitivo de Propósito

Se usa para explicar **para qué** o **por qué** alguien hace algo.

🏗️ Estructura (To + Verbo)

En español solemos decir "para...", en inglés solo usamos TO.

Ejemplo: "I went to the store to buy bread."

(Fui a la tienda PARA comprar pan)

⚠️ Nota importante

¡No uses "for" antes del verbo! Es un error común.

Correcto: To learn (Incorrecto: For learn).

title: Express Agreement (So do I / Neither do I)
description: Agreeing with positive and negative statements
theory:
⚖️ Gerundios vs Infinitivos (A2)

Algunos verbos en inglés requieren que el siguiente verbo vaya en -ing o con to.

📋 Lista Rápida
  • Verb + -ing: Enjoy, Finish, Avoid, Suggest. "I enjoy dancing."
  • Verb + to: Want, Need, Decide, Plan. "I want to go."
📝 Ejemplos

"I decided to study English."

"She finished reading the book."

title: Stative Verbs
description: Verbs that describe states, not actions
theory:
🚶 Preposiciones de Movimiento

Indican la dirección en la que alguien o algo se está moviendo.

🗺️ Direcciones Comunes
  • Into: Hacia adentro. "Go into the house."
  • Out of: Hacia afuera. "Get out of the car."
  • Through: A través de. "Walk through the park."
  • Towards: Hacia. "Run towards the goal."
💡 Otras útiles

Up / Down (Arriba/Abajo), Over (Por encima), Past (Pasando por delante).

title: Passive Voice (Simple Forms)
description: Focus on the action, not the doer
theory:
🧩 Introducción a los Phrasal Verbs

Un Phrasal Verb es la combinación de un **verbo** y una **partícula** (preposición o adverbio) que juntos crean un nuevo significado.

📋 Ejemplos para Empezar
  • Wake up: Despertarse.
  • Get up: Levantarse de la cama.
  • Turn on / off: Encender / Apagar.
  • Sit down / Stand up: Sentarse / Levantarse.
  • Look for: Buscar.
⚠️ El secreto de los Phrasal Verbs

No intentes traducirlos palabra por palabra. Tómalo como una palabra nueva con un significado propio.

title: Indefinite Pronouns
theory:
✨ Algo, Nada, Cualquier cosa: Pronombres Indefinidos

Se usan para referirse a cosas o personas sin decir exactamente quiénes o qué son.

⚖️ Cuándo usar cada uno
  • Something: En oraciones afirmativas. "I have something for you."
  • Anything: En preguntas y negaciones. "Do you have anything?" / "I don't have anything."
  • Nothing: En oraciones afirmativas con sentido negativo. "I have nothing."
📝 Nota importante

En inglés **no existe la doble negación**. No digas "I don't have nothing".

Correcto: "I have nothing" o "I don't have anything".

title: Too vs. Enough
theory:
⚖️ Cantidad y Grado: Too / Enough

Sirven para decir que algo es excesivo o suficiente.

🏗️ Posición en la frase
  • Too + Adjetivo: Demasiado. "It's too hot."
  • Adjetivo + Enough: Suficiente. "It's hot enough."
  • Enough + Sustantivo: Suficiente cantidad. "I have enough money."
📝 Ejemplos

"This shirt is too big for me."

"I am not old enough to drive."

title: So vs. Such
theory:
🏠 Pronombres Posesivos

Se usan para decir de quién es algo sin repetir el sustantivo.

🔗 Adjetivo vs Pronombre
Posesivo Pronombre
My (Mi carro) Mine (Es mío)
Your (Tu carro) Yours (Es tuyo)
His/Her (Su carro) His/Hers (Es suyo)
Our (Nuestro) Ours (Es nuestro)
Their (Su - ellos) Theirs (Es de ellos)
📝 Ejemplo

"Is this your pen?" - "No, it's mine." (No, es el mío)

title: Both, Either, Neither
theory:
🗝️ Posesión: Whose / Genitivo 's

Existen dos formas comunes de preguntar y expresar posesión de personas.

🏗️ Whose (¿De quién?)

"Whose phone is this?" (¿De quién es este teléfono?)

📝 Genitivo Sajón ('s)

Añadimos 's al poseedor. No usamos "de".

"It is John's phone." (Es el teléfono de John)

"My mother's car." (El carro de mi madre)

title: Adverbs for Possibility
theory:
🔗 Cláusulas Relativas: Who, Which, That

Sirven para unir dos frases y dar más información sobre alguien o algo.

📋 ¿Cuál elegir?
  • Who: Para personas. "The man who lives next door."
  • Which: Para cosas o animales. "The car which is red."
  • That: Para ambos (en contextos informales).
📝 Ejemplo

"I met a girl who speaks five languages."

"This is the book that I like."

title: Modals for Prohibition
theory:
🗺️ Pidiendo y Dando Direcciones

Saber orientarse y preguntar cómo llegar es una habilidad de supervivencia en inglés.

🗣️ Preguntas Útiles
  • Excuse me, where is the...?
  • How can I get to...?
  • Is it far from here?
🧭 Instrucciones
  • Go straight: Sigue derecho.
  • Turn left / right: Gira a la izquierda / derecha.
  • Take the first turning: Toma la primera salida.
  • Go past the bank: Pasa el banco.

title: Second Conditional
theory:
🍽️ En el Restaurante (At the Restaurant)

Frases clave para pedir comida y tener una experiencia agradable.

📝 Al Pedir
  • A table for two, please.
  • Can I see the menu?
  • I would like (I'd like)... (La forma más educada de pedir)
  • Can I have the bill, please? (¿Me trae la cuenta?)
💡 Vocabulario
  • Starter: Entrada.
  • Main course: Plato fuerte.
  • Dessert: Postre.

title: First vs Second Conditional
theory:
✈️ Viajes y Transporte (Traveling)

Vocabulario para aeropuertos, trenes y moverte por el mundo.

🚉 Medios de Transporte
  • By plane / By train / By bus.
  • On foot: Caminando.
  • Ticket office / Boarding pass.
🗣️ Frases Útiles

"What time does the train leave?"

"A single ticket to London, please."

title: Past Modals
description: Should have, could have, would have
theory:
🤒 Salud y Enfermedades (Health)

Expresar cómo te sientes físicamente es vital.

🏥 Dolores Comunes
  • Headache: Dolor de cabeza.
  • Stomach ache: Dolor de estómago.
  • Backache: Dolor de espalda.
  • Sore throat: Dolor de garganta.
🗣️ ¿Cómo explicarlo?

"I have a cold." (Tengo un resfriado)

"I feel dizzy." (Me siento mareado)

title: Reported Speech (Statements & Requests)
theory:
☀️ El Clima y las Estaciones

El tema de conversación universal por excelencia.

🌤️ Estados del Tiempo
  • Sunny / Cloudy: Soleado / Nublado.
  • Rainy / Snowy: Lluvioso / Nevado.
  • Windy / Foggy: Ventoso / Con niebla.
🍂 Las Estaciones
  • Spring: Primavera.
  • Summer: Verano.
  • Autumn (Fall): Otoño.
  • Winter: Invierno.

title: Reported Speech (Modals)
theory:
🏁 Repaso General A2

¡Felicidades! Has cubierto la gramática base necesaria para ser un usuario independiente en inglés.

🚀 Habilidades que ya tienes
  • Puedes hablar del pasado con fluidez.
  • Puedes comparar objetos y personas.
  • Sabes expresar obligación, permiso y consejos.
  • Puedes pedir comida, direcciones y viajar solo.
🔜 ¿Qué sigue?

El nivel B1 (Intermedio) te espera, donde aprenderás el Presente Perfecto, la Voz Pasiva y a expresar opiniones mucho más complejas.

title: Relative Pronouns
description: Master defining and non-defining relative clauses
theory:
✨ Presente Perfecto (Introducción)

Se usa para hablar de **experiencias** en nuestra vida sin decir exactamente cuándo ocurrieron, o acciones que empezaron en el pasado y continúan hoy.

🏗️ Estructura (Have / Has + Participio)
  • I / You / We / They + have + eaten.
  • He / She / It + has + eaten.
📝 Ejemplos Clave

"I have traveled to Italy." (He viajado a Italia - Experiencia)

"She has lost her keys." (Ha perdido sus llaves - Resultado ahora)

title: Gerund Phrases as Subjects
description: Use gerunds to start sentences naturally
theory:
⏳ For vs Since

Ambas se usan con el Presente Perfecto para indicar cuánto tiempo lleva ocurriendo algo.

⚖️ La Diferencia
  • FOR: Indica un **periodo de tiempo** (duración). For 2 hours, For 5 years.
  • SINCE: Indica un **punto específico** en el tiempo (inicio). Since 1990, Since Monday, Since I was a child.
📝 Ejemplos

"I have lived here for ten years."

"I have lived here since 2014."

title: Gerunds and Infinitives
description: Know which verbs take -ing or to + verb
theory:
🕒 Just, Already, Yet

Estas palabras dan información extra sobre el tiempo en el Presente Perfecto.

📋 ¿Cómo usarlos?
  • Just: Acaba de ocurrir. "I've just finished."
  • Already: Ya ocurrió (antes de lo esperado). "I've already eaten."
  • Yet: Todavía no (en negaciones y preguntas). "I haven't finished yet." / "Have you finished yet?"
⚠️ Posición

Just y Already van entre el auxiliar 'have' y el verbo. Yet siempre va al final de la frase.

title: Describing Problems 1
description: Talk about damage using adjectives and nouns
theory:
⚖️ Present Perfect vs Past Simple

Es la duda más común en el nivel B1. La clave está en el **tiempo**.

🔍 Diferencia Crítica
  • Past Simple: Tiempo terminado y específico. "I saw him yesterday."
  • Present Perfect: Tiempo no terminado o no mencionado. "I have seen him before."
💡 Palabras que definen el tiempo

Past Simple: Yesterday, In 1990, Last week.

Present Perfect: Ever, Never, So far, Already.

title: Describing Problems 2
theory:
🔄 Voz Pasiva (Presente)

Se usa cuando el objeto de la acción es más importante que quien la realiza, o cuando no sabemos quién la hizo.

🏗️ Estructura (Am/Is/Are + Participio)

Activa: "Farmers grow coffee in Brazil."

Pasiva: "Coffee is grown in Brazil."

📝 ¿Cuándo usarla?

Para procesos, noticias o hechos generales donde el "actor" es irrelevante.

"The office is cleaned every day."

title: Reported Requests
theory:
🏛️ Voz Pasiva (Pasado)

Muy común para hablar de historia, arte o crímenes pasados.

🏗️ Estructura (Was/Were + Participio)

Activa: "Shakespeare wrote Romeo and Juliet."

Pasiva: "Romeo and Juliet was written by Shakespeare."

💡 El agente: BY

Si quieres mencionar quién hizo la acción, usa la palabra BY.

"The car was repaired by a mechanic."

title: Reported Commands
theory:
Theory: Relative Pronouns

Relative pronouns connect clauses to describe a noun. In B1, understanding the types of clauses and omission rules is key.

1. Defining vs Non-Defining Clauses
Type Function Punctuation "That"?
Defining Identifies exactly which person/thing we mean.
"The man who helps me is nice."		 No commas ✅ Yes
Non-Defining Adds extra information. The sentence works without it.
"My dad, who helps everyone, is nice."		 Must use commas (brackets) ❌ NEVER
2. The Pronouns & Usage
  • Who: People. (The girl who called.)
  • Which: Things/Animals. (The car which I drove.)
  • That: People/Things (Informal, Defining clauses ONLY).
  • Whose: Possession (His/Her/Its). (The boy whose bike was stolen.)
  • Where: Places. (The city where I live.)
🚀 Advanced Rule: Omission

You can delete the pronoun (who/which/that) IF it is the object of the verb.

  • - "The book (which) I read is good." (I read the book → Object → Optional)
  • - "The man who lives here." (He lives → Subject → Required)

title: Quantifiers
theory:
⚖️ Zero Conditional (Hechos)

Se usa para verdades universales, hechos científicos o situaciones que siempre son ciertas.

🏗️ Estructura (If Present, Present)

"If you heat ice, it melts."

"If I drink too much coffee, I don't sleep."

💡 Sustitución por "When"

En el Zero Conditional, casi siempre puedes cambiar "If" por "When" sin perder el sentido.

title: Narrative Tenses
description: Tell stories in the past effectively
theory:
🚀 First Conditional (Posibilidad Real)

Se usa para hablar de situaciones futuras que son muy probables si se cumple una condición.

🏗️ Estructura (If Present, Will)

"If it rains tomorrow, I will stay home."

"If you study, you will pass the exam."

⚠️ Nota importante

¡Nunca pongas "will" en la parte del "If"!

Correcto: If I win... (Incorrecto: If I will win...).

title: Noun Phrases
theory:
💭 Second Conditional (Imaginario)

Se usa para situaciones hipotéticas, sueños o consejos (cosas poco probables o irreales).

🏗️ Estructura (If Past, Would)

"If I won the lottery, I would buy a mansion."

"If I were you, I would call her."

⚠️ El uso de "WERE"

En el Second Conditional, solemos usar WERE para todos los sujetos, incluyendo I, He, She, It (en lugar de 'was').

Ejemplo: "If she were rich..."

title: Future Forms Review
description: Master all ways to talk about the future
theory:
🕵️ Modales de Deducción (Presente)

Se usan cuando hacemos una suposición basada en la evidencia que tenemos.

🔍 Niveles de Certeza
  • Must (90-100% seguro): "He must be at work. His car is gone."
  • Might / Could / May (50% seguro): "It might rain later."
  • Can't (90-100% seguro de que NO): "He can't be rich. He has no money."
⚠️ ¡Nota importante!

No usamos "mustn't" para deducciones negativas. Usamos can't.

title: Passives Review
theory:
⚖️ Cuantificadores: Few, Little, Many, Much

Saber elegir el cuantificador correcto depende de si el sustantivo es contable o incontable.

📊 Mapa de Cantidades
Sentido Contables (Plural) Incontables
Mucho Many Much
Poco Few / A few Little / A little
💡 Diferencia Sutil

A few / A little: Sentido positivo (un poco, algo). "I have a little money."

Few / Little: Sentido negativo (casi nada). "I have little money."

title: Purpose Clauses
theory:
🪞 Pronombres Reflexivos

Se usan cuando el sujeto y el objeto de la oración son la misma persona.

🏗️ La Lista de Espejos
  • I → Myself.
  • You → Yourself / Yourselves.
  • He → Himself.
  • She → Herself.
  • It → Itself.
  • We → Ourselves.
  • They → Themselves.
📝 Ejemplos

"I cut myself while cooking."

"She taught herself English."

title: Verbs with Prepositions
theory:
💬 Question Tags (¿Verdad?)

Son pequeñas preguntas al final de una frase para confirmar información o buscar acuerdo.

⚖️ Regla de los Opuestos
  • Frase (+) → Tag (-): "You are tired, aren't you?"
  • Frase (-) → Tag (+): "You don't smoke, do you?"
🔍 Caso Especial

Con "I am", el tag es aren't I?

"I am late, aren't I?"

title: Future Perfect
theory:
🤝 Preguntas Indirectas (Cortesía)

Se usan para sonar más educado o formal al pedir información.

🏗️ El Cambio de Orden

Cuando la pregunta está dentro de otra frase, el orden vuelve a ser normal (Sujeto + Verbo).

Directa: "Where is the bank?"

Indirecta: "Can you tell me where the bank is?" (No: "where is the bank").

💡 Frases de Apertura
  • "Do you know...?"
  • "Could you tell me...?"
  • "I was wondering if..."

title: Get or Have Something Done
theory:
🗣️ Reported Speech (Estilo Indirecto)

Se usa para contar lo que otra persona dijo. En inglés, solemos "dar un paso atrás" en el tiempo gramatical.

⏳ El Cambio de Tiempos
  • Presente Simple → Pasado Simple.
  • Presente Continuo → Pasado Continuo.
  • Will → Would.
  • Can → Could.
📝 Ejemplo

Directo: "I am happy."

Reported: "He said that he was happy."

title: Should Have & Suggestions
theory:
✨ Intensificadores: So vs Such

Ambos significan "tan", pero se usan con estructuras diferentes.

⚖️ Diferencia de Uso
  • SO + Adjetivo / Adverbio: "He is so tall."
  • SUCH + (Adjetivo) + Sustantivo: "He is such a tall man."
📝 Ejemplos

"The movie was so good."

"It was such a good movie."

title: Would Rather vs Would Prefer
theory:
⚖️ Both, Neither, Either

Se usan para hablar de **dos** personas o cosas.

📋 ¿Cómo elegir?
  • Both: Los dos. "Both John and Mary are here."
  • Neither: Ninguno de los dos. "Neither of them is ready."
  • Either: Cualquiera de los dos (uno u otro). "You can have either tea or coffee."
🔗 Las Parejas Clave

Neither va con nor. | Either va con or.

title: Third Conditional
theory:
🔄 Habituación: Get used to / Be used to

No lo confundas con "Used to" (hábito pasado). Estos hablan de **acostumbrarse**.

🏗️ Las Dos Formas
  • Be used to: Ya estás acostumbrado. "I am used to the cold."
  • Get used to: Estás en proceso de acostumbrarte. "I am getting used to my new job."
⚠️ Nota Gramatical

Después de estas estructuras, siempre usamos un **Sustantivo** o un **Verbo con -ing**.

"I am used to driving on the left."

title: Time Clauses
theory:
🕒 Pasado Continuo

Se usa para acciones que estaban en progreso en un momento específico del pasado.

🏗️ Estructura (Was/Were + -ing)
  • I was studying.
  • They were playing.
💡 Uso con el Pasado Simple

Solemos usar el Pasado Continuo para la acción larga y el Pasado Simple para la interrupción.

"I was watching TV when the phone rang."

title: Clauses of Purpose
theory:
🕒 Pasado Perfecto (El "pasado del pasado")

Se usa para hablar de una acción que ocurrió **antes** de otra acción en el pasado.

🏗️ Estructura (Had + Participio)

Sujeto + had + Verbo en 3ra columna.

Ejemplo: "When I arrived, the train had left."

(Cuando llegué, el tren ya se había ido).

💡 Uso con "Already"

Es muy común usarlo con 'already' para enfatizar que algo ya había terminado.

"I had already eaten when they invited me."

title: Question Tags
theory:
🌠 Expresar Deseos: Wish (Presente)

Se usa para expresar que queremos que una situación presente sea diferente (deseos imaginarios).

🏗️ Estructura (Wish + Pasado)

Aunque el deseo es para el presente, usamos el verbo en **pasado**.

  • "I wish I had more money." (Desearía tener más dinero).
  • "I wish I were taller." (Desearía ser más alto).
⚠️ Nota sobre "Were"

Al igual que en el condicional, es más común y correcto usar were para todos los sujetos (I, He, She, It).

title: Modals of Deduction
theory:
🏃 Adverbios de Modo (Adverbs of Manner)

Describen **cómo** se realiza una acción.

📋 Regla General (-LY)

Se forman añadiendo -ly al adjetivo.

  • Quick → Quickly (Rápidamente).
  • Careful → Carefully (Cuidadosamente).
  • Happy → Happily (Felizmente).
🌟 Irregulares
  • Good → Well. "He plays well."
  • Fast → Fast. "He runs fast."
  • Hard → Hard. "He works hard."

title: Past Modals of Deduction
description: Make logical conclusions about the past
theory:
🧩 Phrasal Verbs (Intermedio)

En este nivel, nos enfocamos en Phrasal Verbs que son muy comunes en el habla diaria y profesional.

📋 Lista de Supervivencia
  • Find out: Descubrir información.
  • Set up: Configurar o establecer.
  • Give up: Rendirse o dejar un hábito.
  • Pick up: Recoger a alguien o algo.
  • Grow up: Crecer (persona).
📝 Ejemplos

"I need to find out what happened."

"Don't give up on your dreams!"

title: Reported Speech
theory:
🗣️ Dar Opiniones (Giving Opinions)

Aprende a expresar tu punto de vista de forma variada y natural.

📋 Frases para Empezar
  • In my opinion...
  • From my point of view...
  • As far as I'm concerned... (Más formal).
  • To be honest... (Para ser honesto).
🤝 Estar de acuerdo o en desacuerdo

Acuerdo: "I totally agree with you."

Desacuerdo: "I'm afraid I disagree." (Muy educado).

title: Giving Reasons
theory:
🌟 Describiendo Experiencias

Usa una mezcla de Presente Perfecto y Pasado Simple para contar historias interesantes.

🏗️ El flujo de la historia

1. Introduce con Presente Perfecto: "I have been to Japan."

2. Da detalles con Pasado Simple: "I went there in 2019 and I ate amazing sushi."

💡 Adjetivos para Impactar

Usa palabras como amazing, life-changing, challenging, unforgettable.

title: Past Modals (Judgments)
theory:
🔮 Hacer Predicciones

Cómo hablar de lo que crees que pasará en el futuro con diferentes niveles de certeza.

📊 Niveles de Certeza
  • Will: Predicción general. "I think it will rain."
  • Going to: Basado en evidencia. "Look at those clouds, it's going to rain."
  • Probably: "He will probably win."
📝 Estructuras de Pensamiento

"I believe that..." | "I expect that..." | "I doubt that..."

title: Past Accomplishments
theory:
🤝 Socializar y Networking

Frases para romper el hielo y mantener conversaciones fluidas en eventos sociales.

🗣️ Romper el Hielo (Icebreakers)
  • "So, what brings you here?"
  • "Have you been here before?"
  • "How do you know the host?"
💡 Seguir la Conversación

Usa Follow-up questions: "¿En serio? ¿Y qué pasó después?" (Really? And what happened then?).

title: Passive Process
theory:
👔 Introducción al Business English

El lenguaje que necesitas para sonar profesional en una oficina o entrevista.

💼 Vocabulario Corporativo
  • Colleagues: Compañeros de trabajo.
  • Deadline: Fecha límite.
  • Feedback: Retroalimentación.
  • Meeting / Briefing: Reunión.
🗣️ Frases Profesionales

"Let's schedule a meeting."

"I'll get back to you on that." (Te daré una respuesta luego).

title: Future Perfect
theory:
🏁 Repaso General B1

¡Increíble! Has alcanzado el nivel intermedio. Ahora eres un usuario independiente del inglés.

🚀 Tus nuevos superpoderes
  • Puedes hablar de experiencias pasadas con tiempos perfectos.
  • Entiendes y usas la voz pasiva.
  • Puedes plantear situaciones hipotéticas con condicionales.
  • Tienes herramientas para el entorno laboral y social.
🔜 ¿Qué sigue?

El nivel B2 (Intermedio Alto) te llevará a la fluidez total, con estructuras narrativas avanzadas y gramática de precisión.

title: Verbs + Gerunds
description: Master verbs that take gerund forms
theory:
⏳ Presente Perfecto Continuo

Se usa para acciones que comenzaron en el pasado y continúan en el presente, enfatizando la **duración** o el **proceso**.

🏗️ Estructura (Have/Has + Been + -ing)
  • "I have been studying all day." (He estado estudiando todo el día).
  • "She has been working here for years."
💡 ¿Cuándo usarlo?

1. Para acciones que acaban de terminar y tienen un resultado visible ahora (ej: estás sudado porque has estado corriendo).

2. Para acciones temporales que han estado ocurriendo últimamente.

title: Noun Clauses
description: Use noun clauses to explain and define
theory:
🕒 Pasado Perfecto Continuo

Se usa para hablar de una acción larga que estaba ocurriendo **antes** de otro punto específico en el pasado.

🏗️ Estructura (Had + Been + -ing)

"The ground was wet because it had been raining." (El suelo estaba mojado porque había estado lloviendo).

"I had been waiting for two hours when she arrived."

⚠️ Enfasis en la Causa

A diferencia del Pasado Perfecto simple (que enfoca en el resultado), el continuo enfoca en la duración de la actividad que causó el resultado.

title: Past Modals
theory:
💭 Tercer Condicional (El pasado imposible)

Se usa para imaginar cómo habrían sido las cosas si el pasado hubiera sido diferente. Habla de arrepentimientos o críticas.

🏗️ Estructura (If Past Perfect, Would Have + Participio)

"If I had studied more, I would have passed."

(Si hubiera estudiado más, habría aprobado).

📝 Ejemplos

"If we had taken a taxi, we wouldn't have missed the flight."

title: Modal Uses
theory:
⚖️ Condicionales Mixtos (Mixed Conditionals)

A veces una condición en el pasado tiene un resultado en el **presente**. Aquí es donde mezclamos tiempos.

🏗️ Estructura Común (If Past Perfect, Would + Base)

"If I had won the lottery, I would be rich now."

(Si hubiera ganado la lotería [pasado], sería rico ahora [presente]).

💡 ¿Por qué usarlos?

Sirven para conectar hechos pasados con nuestra realidad actual.

title: Relative Clauses
theory:
🌠 Wish / If Only (Arrepentimiento Pasado)

Se usa para expresar un deseo de que algo en el pasado hubiera sido diferente.

🏗️ Estructura (Wish + Past Perfect)
  • "I wish I had said something." (Ojalá hubiera dicho algo).
  • "If only I hadn't spent all my money." (Si tan solo no hubiera gastado todo mi dinero).
⚠️ Diferencia con el presente

Recuerda: Wish + Pasado Simple = deseo presente. Wish + Pasado Perfecto = arrepentimiento pasado.

title: Order of Modifiers
theory:
🔄 Voz Pasiva Avanzada

En nivel B2 usamos estructuras pasivas para sonar más objetivos, especialmente en noticias o reportes.

🏗️ Estructuras Impersonales
  • It is said that... (Se dice que...).
  • He is thought to be... (Se piensa que él es...).
  • It is believed that... (Se cree que...).
📝 Ejemplo

"It is believed that the company will close."

"The suspect is thought to have escaped."

title: Passive Voice Past
theory:
🛠️ Causativo: Have/Get something done

Se usa cuando otra persona realiza un servicio para nosotros, usualmente porque le pagamos o se lo pedimos.

🏗️ Estructura (Have + Objeto + Participio)

"I had my hair cut yesterday." (Me corté el pelo - alguien más lo hizo).

"She is getting her car repaired." (Le están arreglando el carro).

⚠️ Error común

No digas "I cut my hair" a menos que tú mismo hayas tomado las tijeras y lo hayas hecho.

title: Infinitive Patterns
theory:
🗣️ Reported Speech Avanzado

Ya no solo usamos 'say' o 'tell'. En B2 usamos verbos más precisos que indican la intención.

📋 Verbos de Reporte
  • Suggest + -ing: "He suggested going to the beach."
  • Refuse + to: "She refused to help me."
  • Accuse of + -ing: "They accused him of stealing."
📝 Ejemplo

"He apologized for being late." (Él se disculpó por llegar tarde).

title: Noun-forming Suffixes
theory:
🕵️ Modales de Deducción (Pasado)

Se usan para hacer suposiciones sobre cosas que ya pasaron.

🔍 Grados de Certeza
  • Must have (Seguro): "He must have forgotten." (Debió habérsele olvidado).
  • Might have (Posible): "I might have left it at home."
  • Can't have (Imposible): "You can't have seen him; he's in Tokyo!"
⚠️ Nota

Usamos "could have" para posibilidades pasadas que no ocurrieron. "You could have died!"

title: Tag Questions
theory:
🚀 Futuro Continuo

Se usa para una acción que estará en progreso en un momento determinado del futuro.

🏗️ Estructura (Will be + -ing)

"This time tomorrow, I will be lying on the beach."

(Mañana a esta hora estaré acostado en la playa).

💡 Uso de Cortesía

También se usa para preguntar planes de forma educada: "Will you be using the car later?"

title: Adjective-forming Suffixes
theory:
🏁 Futuro Perfecto

Se usa para decir que algo estará **terminado** antes de un punto específico en el futuro.

🏗️ Estructura (Will have + Participio)

"I will have finished the report by 5 PM."

(Habré terminado el reporte para las 5 PM).

📅 Palabra clave: BY

Casi siempre usamos "By" (para tal hora/fecha) con el Futuro Perfecto.

title: Reported Speech
theory:
📖 Tiempos Narrativos (Narrative Tenses)

Para contar una gran historia, necesitamos combinar todos los pasados que conocemos.

🎭 Los 4 Protagonistas
  • Past Simple: Acciones principales. "I went out."
  • Past Continuous: Escenario/Fondo. "The sun was shining."
  • Past Perfect: Hechos anteriores. "I had forgotten my umbrella."
  • Past Perfect Continuous: Duración previa. "It had been raining for days."

title: Participial Adjectives
theory:
🔗 Cláusulas de Participio

Se usan para acortar frases y dar un tono más literario o académico.

🏗️ Estructura (-ing / Participio)
  • Present Participle: "Feeling tired, she went to bed early." (Como se sentía cansada...)
  • Past Participle: "Born in 1990, he grew up in Madrid."
  • Perfect Participle: "Having finished the test, he left."

title: Unreal Conditionals
theory:
🔀 Inversión (Introducción)

La inversión consiste en poner el auxiliar antes del sujeto en frases que **no** son preguntas. Se usa para dar énfasis o un tono formal.

🏗️ Cuándo usarla
  • Expresiones Negativas: Never, Seldom, Rarely. "Never have I seen such a mess."
  • Only after / Not only: "Not only did he arrive late, but he also forgot the cake."
⚠️ Tono Formal

La inversión es muy común en la literatura y en discursos formales. En el habla diaria es poco frecuente pero demuestra un nivel C1/C2.

title: Indirect Questions
theory:
✨ Cleft Sentences (Frases Hendidas)

Se usan para poner el foco en una parte específica de la oración. Es como usar un "resaltador" en tu lenguaje.

🏗️ Estructuras Comunes
  • IT is/was...: "It was John who broke the window." (No fue otro, fue John).
  • WHAT...: "What I need is a coffee." (Lo que necesito es...).
📝 Comparación

Normal: "I love your smile."

Cleft: "What I love is your smile."

title: Gerunds vs Infinitives
theory:
⚖️ Gerundios vs Infinitivos (Avanzado)

En nivel B2, estudiamos verbos que cambian totalmente de significado dependiendo de si usamos -ing o to.

📋 Verbos que Cambian
  • Remember: Doing: Recordar un recuerdo pasado. To do: Recordar una tarea pendiente.
  • Stop: Doing: Dejar de hacer un hábito. To do: Detenerse para empezar otra cosa.
  • Try: Doing: Experimentar. To do: Hacer un esfuerzo difícil.

title: Other Uses of To
theory:
🧩 Phrasal Verbs (Nivel B2)

Dominar estos verbos es lo que te hará sonar realmente fluido y natural.

📋 Phrasal Verbs de Alto Impacto
  • Look forward to: Estar ansioso por algo (lleva -ing después).
  • Put up with: Tolerar.
  • Run out of: Quedarse sin algo.
  • Make up: Inventar o reconciliarse.
  • Take after: Parecerse a un familiar.
📝 Ejemplo

"I can't put up with this noise anymore!"

title: Wish & If Only
theory:
🎭 Idioms y Expresiones Idiomáticas

Los Idioms son frases cuyo significado no se puede deducir de las palabras individuales. Son esenciales para el inglés coloquial.

📋 Expresiones Populares
  • Under the weather: Sentirse mal de salud.
  • Break a leg: ¡Buena suerte! (en teatro).
  • A piece of cake: Algo muy fácil.
  • The elephant in the room: Un problema obvio que nadie menciona.
⚠️ Uso con precaución

No abuses de los Idioms en contextos extremadamente formales (como reportes científicos).

title: Perfect Modals
theory:
🔗 Conectores y Linking Words

Son el "pegamento" de tus ideas. Sin ellos, tu inglés suena como una lista de supermercado.

📋 Clasificación
  • Contraste: However, Nevertheless, Although, Despite.
  • Añadir info: Furthermore, Moreover, In addition.
  • Causa/Efecto: Consequently, Therefore, Due to.
📝 Ejemplo

"The test was hard. However, I managed to pass."

title: Subordinating Conjunctions
theory:
📝 Escritura Académica (Intro)

Cómo estructurar ensayos, reportes y correos profesionales con un tono formal.

🏗️ Las Reglas de Oro
  • No contracciones: Usa "do not" en lugar de "don't".
  • Vocabulario preciso: Usa "consume" en lugar de "eat".
  • Voz pasiva: Para sonar objetivo.
💡 Estructura de Párrafo

Topic Sentence → Supporting sentences → Concluding sentence.

title: Present Perfect Continuous
theory:
🤝 Negociación y Persuasión

Habilidades de comunicación para llegar a acuerdos y convencer a otros en inglés.

🗣️ Frases de Poder
  • "What if we..." (¿Y si...).
  • "I'm willing to... if you..." (Estoy dispuesto a... si tú...).
  • "That seems fair." (Eso parece justo).
  • "Could we find a middle ground?" (¿Podríamos encontrar un punto medio?).

title: Used To Structures
theory:
🎤 Habilidades de Presentación

Cómo estructurar una charla en público y mantener a la audiencia interesada.

🏗️ Partes de la Presentación
  • Opening: "I'd like to start by..."
  • Transitions: "Moving on to my next point..."
  • Closing: "To wrap up..."
💡 Consejos

Mantén contacto visual, usa un lenguaje sencillo y haz preguntas retóricas.

title: Past Perfect Continuous
description: Ongoing actions before a past moment
theory:
🧠 Pensamiento Crítico en Inglés

Aprende a analizar argumentos, detectar falacias y expresar razonamientos complejos.

🗣️ Lenguaje Analítico
  • "This suggests that..." (Esto sugiere que...).
  • "An alternative explanation is..." (Una explicación alternativa es...).
  • "Evidence shows that..." (La evidencia muestra que...).

title: Future Perfect Continuous
description: Duration up to a future point
theory:
🌍 Matices Culturales

Hablar inglés no es solo gramática; es entender la cortesía, el sarcasmo y las normas sociales de los países angloparlantes.

📋 Temas Clave
  • Politeness: El uso excesivo de "Sorry" y "Please" en UK.
  • Small Talk: Temas seguros (clima, hobbies) vs prohibidos (religión, salario).
  • Directness: Americanos (más directos) vs Británicos (más indirectos/vagos).

title: Mixed Conditionals
description: Combine past and present in conditionals
theory:
🏁 Repaso General B2

¡Increíble! Has alcanzado el umbral de la fluidez profesional. Ahora eres un usuario competente capaz de trabajar y estudiar en inglés.

🚀 Tus Logros
  • Manejas tiempos narrativos complejos.
  • Entiendes matices culturales y dobles sentidos.
  • Puedes debatir y negociar con argumentos sólidos.
  • Tu escritura es estructurada y profesional.
🔝 ¿Qué sigue?

El nivel C1 (Avanzado) te espera para pulir los últimos detalles y alcanzar la maestría total del idioma.

title: Phrasal Verbs
description: Master complex multi-word verbs for natural expression
theory:
🧩 Phrasal Verbs (Nivel C1)

En el nivel C1, el enfoque cambia hacia Phrasal Verbs con múltiples significados metafóricos y aquellos usados en contextos académicos o literarios.

📋 Phrasal Verbs de Precisión
  • Account for: Explicar o justificar. "How do you account for this error?"
  • Bear out: Confirmar o respaldar. "The evidence bears out my theory."
  • Bring about: Causar que algo suceda. "The new law brought about many changes."
  • Set forth: Exponer o presentar ideas. "He set forth his arguments in the meeting."
⚠️ Matices de Registro

Muchos Phrasal Verbs tienen un sinónimo de una sola palabra más formal (ej: Set up → Establish). Un estudiante C1 debe saber cuándo usar cada uno.

title: Gerund & Infinitive
description: Understand meaning changes with gerund vs infinitive
theory:
🔀 Inversión Avanzada

La inversión no es solo gramática; es una herramienta estilística para discursos, literatura y escritura académica de alto nivel.

🏗️ Estructuras Complejas
  • Under no circumstances: "Under no circumstances should you open that door."
  • Little did he know: "Little did he know that his life was about to change."
  • Had I... / Should you...: Inversión en condicionales. "Had I known the truth, I wouldn't have come."
📝 Impacto del énfasis

Normal: "I have never seen such beauty."

Inverted: "Never have I seen such beauty." (Mucho más poderoso y dramático).

title: -ing Clauses
description: Combine ideas fluently with participle clauses
theory:
🚀 Condicionales Avanzados

Más allá del "If", el nivel C1 utiliza estructuras alternativas para expresar condiciones complejas y matizadas.

🏗️ Alternativas al "IF"
  • Provided / Providing that: Siempre y cuando. "You can go, providing that you finish."
  • Unless: A menos que. "I won't go unless you come."
  • But for: Si no fuera por. "But for your help, I would have failed."
  • Were it not for: Si no fuera por (hipotético presente).
💡 Omisión del IF

En contextos formales, omitimos el 'if' e invertimos el sujeto: "Were they to arrive late, the meeting would start without them."

title: Verb Patterns
description: Master sophisticated verb patterns for precision
theory:
🏛️ Voz Pasiva (Nivel C1)

En este nivel, la pasiva se usa para manejar el flujo de información y mantener un tono impersonal en textos complejos.

🏗️ Pasiva con Verbos de Percepción
  • It is rumored to be... (Se rumorea que es...).
  • The results are understood to be... (Se entiende que los resultados son...).
  • Double Passive: "The decision is considered to have been made in haste."
📝 Ejemplo Académico

"The phenomenon has long been observed to occur under specific conditions."

title: Cleft Sentences
description: Add emphasis by splitting sentences with what-clauses
theory:
✨ Cleft Sentences Avanzadas

Las frases hendidas permiten diseccionar una oración para poner el foco exactamente donde el hablante desea.

🏗️ Variaciones de Enfoque
  • The thing that...: "The thing that bothers me is his attitude."
  • The reason why...: "The reason why I called was to apologize."
  • All I want is...: "All I want is a little peace and quiet."
📝 Ejemplo de Contraste

"It wasn't the money I was worried about, it was the time."

title: Compound Adjectives
description: Build rich descriptions with hyphenated adjectives
theory:
⚖️ El Modo Subjuntivo (Subjunctive Mood)

Aunque es raro en el habla común, el subjuntivo es vital en el inglés formal para expresar urgencia, sugerencia o importancia.

🏗️ Estructura (Verbo en infinitivo sin 'to')

Se usa después de verbos como insist, suggest, recommend, demand.

"I suggest that he be told immediately." (No: "is told").

"It is essential that she arrive on time." (No: "arrives").

⚠️ Nota

El subjuntivo no cambia para la tercera persona (no lleva 's').

title: Reporting Clauses
description: Report speech and ideas with advanced structures
theory:
💭 Pasado Irreal (Unreal Past)

Estructuras que usan tiempos pasados para hablar de situaciones hipotéticas o deseos en el presente o futuro.

🏗️ Estructuras Clave
  • I'd rather: Preferiría. "I'd rather you didn't smoke here."
  • It's high time: Ya va siendo hora. "It's high time you started working."
  • As if / As though: Como si. "He talks as if he were the boss."

title: Reporting in Passive
description: Use passive structures for formal reporting
theory:
✂️ Elipsis y Sustitución

Son técnicas para evitar la repetición innecesaria, lo cual es la marca de un hablante avanzado y elegante.

🏗️ Mecanismos de Cohesión
  • Ellipsis: Omitir palabras. "I went to the store and [I] bought some milk."
  • Substitution: Usar 'one/ones', 'do/did', 'so/not'.
  • Ejemplo: "Do you think it will rain?" - "I hope not." (En lugar de "I hope it won't rain").
📝 Ejemplo

"I have a blue pen. Do you have one?"

title: Sentence Adverbs
description: Use adverbs to comment on entire sentences
theory:
📦 Cláusulas Nominales (Nominal Clauses)

Son grupos de palabras que funcionan como un solo sustantivo dentro de la oración.

🏗️ Tipos de Cláusulas
  • That-clauses: "That he failed the test is surprising."
  • Wh-clauses: "What he said was very interesting."
  • Whether-clauses: "Whether he comes or not doesn't matter."
💡 Uso Académico

Estas estructuras permiten condensar mucha información compleja en el sujeto o el objeto de una frase.

title: Double Comparatives
description: Express proportional change with the...the structures
theory:
🔥 Estructuras Enfáticas

Cómo dar fuerza a tus argumentos sin sonar agresivo, usando recursos gramaticales de precisión.

🏗️ El 'DO' Enfático

Usar el auxiliar en frases afirmativas para enfatizar que algo es cierto.

"I do believe we have a chance." (Realmente creo que...).

💡 Inversión y Adverbios

Uso de palabras como Utterly, Entirely, Thoroughly para potenciar el significado.

title: Will & Would Habits
description: Describe present and past habits naturally
theory:
🎭 Registros Formales e Informales

El nivel C1 implica saber "leer la habitación" y ajustar tu lenguaje a la audiencia y el medio.

📋 Diferencias de Registro
Informal Formal
Get better Improve / Ameliorate
Think about Consider / Contemplate
Kids Children / Offspring
⚠️ El peligro de la inconsistencia

Mezclar registros (usar slang en un reporte formal) es un error crítico que delata un nivel inferior al C1.

title: Neither / Either / Both
theory:
🎯 Vocabulario de Precisión

En el nivel C1, dejamos de usar palabras "comodín" (como good, bad, big) y empezamos a usar términos que capturan el matiz exacto.

📋 Sustituciones de Alto Nivel
  • Very happy: Elated, Thrilled, Ecstatic.
  • Very small: Minute, Infinitesimal, Tiny.
  • Very important: Crucial, Vital, Paramount.
  • Very boring: Tedious, Monotonous, Humdrum.
📝 Ejemplo

"The impact of the new policy was paramount to the success of the project."

title: Expressions with 'Get'
theory:
🎭 Idioms Avanzados

Estas expresiones demuestran un dominio profundo de la cultura y la lengua inglesa.

📋 La Lista de Maestría
  • Back to the drawing board: Empezar de cero tras un fallo.
  • Burning the midnight oil: Trabajar hasta muy tarde.
  • Through thick and thin: En las buenas y en las malas.
  • Take it with a grain of salt: No tomarlo literalmente o con total credibilidad.
📝 Ejemplo

"The proposal failed, so it's back to the drawing board for the marketing team."

title: Ellipsis & Substitution
description: Avoid repetition with ellipsis and substitution
theory:
💼 Comunicación Profesional de Alto Nivel

Cómo liderar reuniones, manejar conflictos y negociar acuerdos complejos con diplomacia y autoridad.

🗣️ Lenguaje Diplomático
  • "I'm not entirely convinced that..." (Suena mejor que "estás equivocado").
  • "Could we explore other avenues?" (¿Podemos ver otras opciones?).
  • "I see your point, however..." (Entiendo tu punto, pero...).
💡 Mitigar el impacto

Uso de adverbios de "suavizado": Slightly, Somewhat, To some extent.

title: -ever Words
description: Use compound adverbs to express generality
theory:
📝 Escritura Académica (Nivel C1)

Producción de textos complejos como ensayos críticos, tesis o reportes técnicos de alta calidad.

🏗️ Cohesión y Coherencia
  • Nominalización: Convertir verbos en sustantivos para sonar más académico (ej: "The implementation" en lugar de "We implemented").
  • Conectores complejos: Notwithstanding, Conversely, Albeit.
💡 Ejemplo de Tono

Básico: "People think the law is good."

C1 Académico: "It is widely contended that the legislation yields positive outcomes."

title: Direct & Indirect Objects
description: Master object placement for clarity and style
theory:
🗣️ Debate Complejo y Argumentación

Habilidad para defender posturas en temas polémicos, refutar argumentos y sintetizar ideas en tiempo real.

🏗️ Estructura del Argumento

1. Claim: Tu postura principal.

2. Warrant: La lógica que conecta la evidencia con tu postura.

3. Counter-argument: Anticipar lo que dirá el otro y neutralizarlo.

📝 Frases de Debate

"While it may be true that X, one must consider Y..."

🧠 Conceptos Abstractos

Aprende a discutir temas filosóficos, sociológicos y psicológicos que no tienen una representación física clara.

📋 Vocabulario Abstracto
  • Resilience: Resiliencia.
  • Integrity: Integridad.
  • Paradigm shift: Cambio de paradigma.
  • Subjectivity: Subjetividad.
💡 Ejemplo

"The current paradigm shift in technology is redefining human interaction."

title: Relative Pronouns
description: Know when relative pronouns are mandatory or optional
theory:
🎨 Análisis de Literatura y Arte

Capacidad para analizar simbolismos, metáforas y el trasfondo histórico de obras culturales.

📋 Lenguaje Analítico
  • Symbolism: Simbolismo.
  • Foreshadowing: Presagio.
  • Imagery: Imágenes sensoriales.
  • Allegory: Alegoría.
📝 Ejemplo

"The author uses imagery to evoke a sense of isolation."

title: As if / As though / Like
description: Make comparisons and hypotheses with precision
theory:
🌍 Problemas Globales Actuales

Discutir temas como el cambio climático, la geopolítica y los derechos humanos con un lenguaje técnico y ético.

📋 Temas Críticos
  • Sustainability: Sostenibilidad.
  • Global governance: Gobernanza global.
  • Digital divide: Brecha digital.
  • Humanitarian crisis: Crisis humanitaria.
practice: {"games": [{"type": "multiple_choice", "title": "It seems like...", "instruction": "Choose the correct hypothetical structure.", "questions": [{"q": "He talks as ______ he were the boss.", "options": ["if", "though", "both are correct"], "a": "both are correct"}, {"q": "It looks ______ it's going to rain.", "options": ["like", "as if", "both are correct"], "a": "both are correct"}, {"q": "She acted as if she ______ me.", "options": ["didn't know", "doesn't know", "hasn't known"], "a": "didn't know"}, {"q": "You look as though you ______ a ghost.", "options": ["had seen", "saw", "see"], "a": "had seen"}, {"q": "They feel like they ______ on holiday.", "options": ["are", "were", "be"], "a": "were"}]}, {"type": "fill_in", "title": "Hypothetical Fill C1", "instruction": "Type: as if, as though, or the correct verb form.", "questions": [{"q": "He behaves ______ he owned the place.", "a": "as if"}, {"q": "You look ______ you haven't slept.", "a": "as though"}, {"q": "It feels ______ summer is over.", "a": "like"}, {"q": "She treated me as if I ______ (be) a child.", "a": "were"}, {"q": "You sound as though you ______ (catch) a cold.", "a": "had caught"}]}]}

title: Subjunctive Verbs
description: Use the subjunctive for formal recommendations and wishes
theory:
🚀 Alcanzando la Fluidez Total (Mastering Fluency)

El paso final: cómo sonar indistinguible de un nativo educado, manejando el ritmo, la entonación y las pausas naturales.

🏗️ Elementos de la Fluidez
  • Intonación: Usar el tono para indicar ironía o énfasis.
  • Fillers naturales: "You know", "I mean", "The thing is..." usados con moderación.
  • Colocaciones: Usar palabras que naturalmente van juntas (ej: Commit a crime, no Do a crime).
practice: {"games": [{"type": "multiple_choice", "title": "It is essential that...", "instruction": "Choose the correct subjunctive form.", "questions": [{"q": "It is important that he ______ here.", "options": ["be", "is", "was"], "a": "be"}, {"q": "I suggest that she ______ harder.", "options": ["study", "studies", "studied"], "a": "study"}, {"q": "It is essential that we ______ on time.", "options": ["arrive", "arrives", "arrived"], "a": "arrive"}, {"q": "He demanded that the door ______ unlocked.", "options": ["be", "is", "was"], "a": "be"}, {"q": "I recommend that he ______ a doctor.", "options": ["see", "sees", "seeing"], "a": "see"}]}, {"type": "fill_in", "title": "Subjunctive Fill C1", "instruction": "Type the base form of the verb (e.g., be, study, go).", "questions": [{"q": "It is mandatory that every student ______ (have) an ID.", "a": "have"}, {"q": "I propose that the meeting ______ (be) postponed.", "a": "be"}, {"q": "The doctor suggested that he ______ (rest).", "a": "rest"}, {"q": "It is crucial that she ______ (not / tell) him.", "a": "not tell"}, {"q": "They requested that we ______ (be) present.", "a": "be"}]}]}

title: Subject-Verb Agreement
description: Match verbs correctly with quantifier subjects
theory:
🏁 Maestría Alcanzada: Repaso C1

¡Felicidades, Maestro/a! Has completado el currículo más avanzado de LevelUp English.

👑 Tu nuevo estatus
  • Eres capaz de entender casi cualquier texto, por complejo que sea.
  • Te expresas con espontaneidad y fluidez sin esfuerzo aparente.
  • Usas el lenguaje con flexibilidad y eficacia para fines sociales, académicos y profesionales.
  • Produces textos claros, bien estructurados y detallados sobre temas complejos.
🌟 Un viaje sin fin

El aprendizaje de un idioma nunca termina. ¡Sigue practicando, leyendo y escuchando contenido nativo para mantener tu nivel en la cima!
